w:r.w:t: Public Beta → V2.0 Roadmap | March – May 2026
ClauseWala — AI Legal Documents for Indian Startups

ClauseWala is an AI-powered legal document platform built specifically for Indian startups. The platform generates India-specific contracts — citing the Indian Contract Act 1872, Companies Act 2013, IT Act 2000, and DPDP Act 2023 — through a two-stage process: clause-by-clause blueprint with risk flags, followed by full formatted PDF/DOCX export.

This PRD defines the development priorities for the next 8 weeks (March 20 – May 20, 2026), taking ClauseWala from its current public beta state to a significantly enhanced V2.0. The priorities are informed by expert input from Yogesh Pai (NLU Jaipur faculty) and direct founder feedback sessions.
The following is already built and live at clausewala.in:
✓ Risk flags per clause in Blueprint mode
Groq API — 2-stage pipeline (Blueprint JSON → Full Doc)
Zod — schema validation across API routes and forms
The V2.0 milestone targets three outcome areas:
Stability — harden the existing beta, fix edge cases, and make the core flow bulletproof
Compliance & Trust — add legal safeguards (disclaimers, consent, attorney-client privilege)
Expansion — unlock new user segments (regional language users, rental agreement users, lawyers)
Priority: Critical — must complete before any new feature work
Validate the complete user journey from document selection through AI generation to export:
Document Selection → Form Fill → Blueprint Generation → Blueprint Review
Iteration Chat → Full Document Expansion → PDF Export → DOCX Export
Auth flow: Sign In → Dashboard → Document history
Zero broken flows in the core generation pipeline
PDF and DOCX exports open correctly in Adobe, Word, Google Docs
Document, Organisation, and Iteration DB models tracked correctly
Groq API rate limit errors return user-friendly messages, not crashes
Priority: Critical — legal protection for ClauseWala
Before any document is downloaded, users must acknowledge:
The generated document is a draft, not final legal advice
ClauseWala is not liable for any legal action if the document is presented in court as-is
The document should be reviewed by a qualified lawyer before execution
Step 1: Modal appears before download with 3-point disclaimer
Step 2: User checks a consent checkbox to unlock the download button
Step 3: Consent timestamp and document ID logged in DB
Step 4: Document downloads with embedded disclaimer footer on every page
Consent is non-bypassable — download button disabled until checked
Disclaimer footer auto-appended to PDF and DOCX exports
Consent log stored with user ID, doc ID, and timestamp
Priority: High — recommended by Yogesh Pai (NLU Jaipur)
Generated NDAs and confidential documents currently lack privilege framing. All generated documents must:
Include a confidentiality notice on the first page stating the document is privileged
NDA templates must auto-include attorney-client privilege language where relevant
Add a 'Confidential' watermark option on exported documents
Add CONFIDENTIAL_HEADER clause to clause-library.ts — auto-injected for NDAs, SHA, Term Sheets
Watermark toggle on export settings (optional, user choice)
Privilege language drawn from Indian Evidence Act, Section 126
Priority: High — unlocks mass market beyond English-first startups

The case for this feature: a rental agreement in Jaipur was rejected because it was in English, not Hindi. Most Indian legal agreements at the ground level operate in regional languages. ClauseWala currently generates only in English.
Integrate Bhashini API (MeitY's official translation layer) for all 22 scheduled Indian languages
Language selection on the document form (before generation)
Blueprint generated and reviewed in English (for accuracy)
Final document generated and exported in selected language
Priority languages for V2.0: Hindi, Kannada, Tamil, Telugu, Marathi, Bengali, Gujarati
Bhashini API integrated and tested for Hindi output on NDA and rental agreement templates
Language picker visible on all document generation forms
Exported PDF renders correct Unicode fonts for the selected language
Priority: High — completes the document lifecycle end-to-end
Currently, ClauseWala generates documents but users must sign them externally. Adding e-signature validation via DigiLocker makes ClauseWala a complete contract platform.
Investigate DigiLocker API access and MeitY partnerships for e-sign integration
Generate a shareable, trackable signing link for counterparties
Log signed status, timestamp, and signer identity in DB
Fallback (if DigiLocker API not available in timeline)
Integrate a third-party Indian e-sign provider (SignDesk, Leegality, or Zoho Sign)
This is a known reference point: DJHawker has a similar e-sign validation feature
Priority: Medium — fastest path to a non-startup user segment
Rental agreements are the highest-volume legal document in India, used by millions of individuals — not just startups. This is a significant TAM expansion.
Add dedicated Rental Agreement template (residential and commercial variants)
Form includes: landlord/tenant details, rent, deposit, duration, escalation clause, maintenance terms
Auto-detect user location to set state-specific stamp duty guidance
Bhashini integration is critical here — rental agreements in Tier 2/3 cities are often in regional language
Add a guidance layer: 'Next Steps after Generation' explaining sub-registrar registration process
State-wise stamp duty calculator (static table for V2.0)
Link to relevant state government portal for registration
5.7 LinkedIn for Lawyers — Discovery Layer (Week 6–8, Exploratory)
Priority: Exploratory — long-term monetization and network play
Under Bar Council of India rules, lawyers cannot directly market their services. However, a discovery/connection layer within ClauseWala can work within these constraints:
Lawyers create a profile: specialization, city, languages, experience
Users who need professional review are shown relevant lawyers (not ads — organic discovery)
Lawyer connects with user via the platform — ClauseWala facilitates, not brokers
This aligns with the mission: founders should enter legal conversations informed
Note: Legal review required before implementation. Consult Yogesh Pai on Bar Council compliance before building.
Priority: Exploratory — potential to be genuinely disruptive

Online stamp papers (via SHCIL or state-specific portals) are legally valid in most Indian states. ClauseWala generating stamp-paper-ready documents would remove the last manual step in contract execution.
Research SHCIL API and state-specific digital stamp paper availability
Add 'Generate on Stamp Paper' option at export stage
Start with Maharashtra and Karnataka (highest startup density)
E2E flow tested, Groq error handling, DB models verified
Disclaimer modal live, consent logging, privilege framing in NDA templates
Bhashini API integrated, Hindi output working for NDA and MOU
Rental agreement template live, sub-registrar guidance added, 7 priority languages
DigiLocker/third-party e-sign integrated, signing link flow working
Lawyer profile MVP designed, Bar Council compliance reviewed
SHCIL research complete, stamp paper flow prototyped for MH + KA
The following will NOT be built in this 8-week window:
Paid plans / monetization layer — beta remains free throughout
Full lawyer marketplace with transactions or fees
AI model switch from Groq (performance first, model flexibility later)
Bhashini API quality for legal text
Manual review of translated output; start with Hindi only, expand after validation
Parallel track with third-party e-sign (Leegality/SignDesk) as fallback
Bar Council compliance for lawyer discovery
Legal review with Yogesh Pai before any feature is built
Groq API rate limits at scale
Implement queue + retry logic; add usage tracking per user
Keep as exploratory; no hard deadline, research-only in Week 7–8
By May 20, 2026, ClauseWala V2.0 should demonstrate:
Zero broken flows in the core generation-to-export pipeline
100% of documents include disclaimer + consent logging
Hindi language output live and tested on at least 5 templates
E-sign integration live with at least one provider
Rental agreement template with 50+ documents generated in first 2 weeks of launch
Lawyer discovery MVP design reviewed and Bar Council compliance confirmed
This roadmap was shaped by inputs from:
Yogesh Pai — NLU Jaipur faculty (legal framework review, attorney-client privilege, language integration rationale, Bar Council guidance)
Rudra — e-signature validation approach and DigiLocker integration suggestion
ClauseWala Public Beta users — early founders using the platform during the beta period
Built for Bharat. Legal should feel like infrastructure.
